title: SEGMENT 17 — iPSCs for drug screening and toxicity testing

lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — iPSCs for drug screening and toxicity testing
A more immediately practical use of iPSCs, and one that's quietly become a major industrial application, is drug screening and toxicity testing.
Here's the problem the pharma industry has always faced.
You want to know if a new drug is going to be toxic to human hearts before you give it to humans.
Historically, you tested it in animals — rats, dogs, monkeys — and inferred.

But animals are different from humans in lots of ways, and some drugs that look fine in animals turn out to be cardiotoxic in humans, leading to expensive late-stage failures or, worse, patient deaths after approval.
Vioxx.
Terfenadine.
Astemizole.
A whole gallery of drugs that caused cardiac arrhythmias in humans which animals hadn't predicted.
With iPSCs, you can do something new.
You differentiate iPSCs into cardiomyocytes — actual human heart cells, in a dish, beating like little hearts.
You expose those cells to your candidate drug.
You watch what happens to their action potentials, their calcium handling, their contraction patterns.
If the drug causes arrhythmias in iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes, it probably will in humans.
If it doesn't, that's a positive sign.

This kind of testing has been formalized into something called the CiPA initiative — Comprehensive In Vitro Proarrhythmia Assay — where iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes are used as part of a standardized panel to predict drug-induced arrhythmia risk.
The FDA accepts CiPA data as part of drug submissions now.
iPSC cardiotoxicity testing has become regulatory infrastructure.
The same logic applies to other tissues.
iPSC-derived hepatocytes are used to predict drug-induced liver toxicity, which is one of the most common reasons drugs fail in clinical trials.
iPSC-derived neurons are used to predict neurotoxicity.
iPSC-derived kidney cells can predict nephrotoxicity.
And here's where it gets really interesting.
You can do this with patient-specific iPSCs.
Suppose you have a patient who had a severe adverse reaction to a drug — say, sudden cardiac arrhythmia.
Was it the drug, or was it the patient's particular genetics?
Take a skin biopsy, make iPSCs, differentiate to cardiomyocytes, expose to the drug.
If the patient's cells, specifically, show the arrhythmia phenotype while healthy controls don't, you've identified that patient's particular susceptibility.
That's the seed of pharmacogenomics — using each patient's own cells to predict their drug response.
For very rare diseases, where you can't ethically run a large clinical trial, iPSC-based drug screening can be the primary way to identify candidate therapies.

You take cells from the rare-disease patient, model the disease in a dish, screen thousands of compounds, find ones that fix the phenotype, and then take the best ones forward into the few patients who exist.
This is approximately what was done for the Milasen story you may have heard about — a custom antisense oligonucleotide drug designed for a single child with a unique genetic disease.
iPSCs were part of the workup.
Personalized medicine, at its most extreme.
Drug screening on iPSCs is, in some ways, the steadiest revenue stream the iPSC industry has.
It's not the headline-grabbing cell therapy story.
But it's reducing late-stage drug failures, sharpening pharmacovigilance, and quietly improving the safety of the entire pharmaceutical industry.
The Yamanaka factors, applied to skin biopsies, scaled into industrial cardiotoxicity testing, are saving lives in a roundabout way every day.
